name: Schedule a Home-Service Appointment (Plumber, Electrician, HVAC)
description: Call a home-service contractor to schedule a diagnostic / repair visit, surface the diagnostic fee + after-hours rates up front, and confirm a tight window so the homeowner isn't waiting all day.

context.when_to_use: User needs a contractor on-site: leaking pipe, electrical problem, AC not cooling, appliance broken, etc. Skill is for ROUTINE scheduling, not emergencies — if water is actively flooding, the user should call directly and use emergency dispatch language.
preconditions: One-sentence description of the issue ('garbage disposal jammed, no power'; 'kitchen sink dripping under cabinet'; 'AC fan not turning on').
Availability windows the homeowner can be on-site.
Whether the homeowner wants the cheapest available slot or the soonest.
Approximate age of the system / item if known (a 25-year-old water heater is a different conversation than a 2-year-old one).

principles: Diagnostic fees and after-hours rates are the most common surprise. Surface them up front.
Tight time windows matter. 'Between 8 AM and 5 PM' eats a homeowner's day. Push for a 2-3 hour window or a 'we'll text 30 min before arrival' guarantee.
Get the technician's name when possible — accountability runs higher when a name is attached.

Authorising the diagnostic visit is NOT the same as authorising the repair. Make this explicit: 'I'll authorise the visit + diagnostic fee, but any repair over $X needs me to approve it before the tech proceeds.'
Watch out for 'membership' upsells at the end. Politely defer ('I'll think about that and call back') unless the homeowner has pre-authorised.

phrases.opener: Hi, I'd like to schedule a service visit at [address]. The issue is [one-line description]. When can someone come out?
phrases.ask_diagnostic_fee: What's your diagnostic or service-call fee? Is it waived if we go forward with the repair?
phrases.ask_after_hours: Is that during regular business hours, or would it be an after-hours rate?
phrases.ask_arrival_window: What's the arrival window — 2-hour, 4-hour? Will the technician call or text before arriving?
phrases.ask_technician_name: Do you know which technician will come out so I can let the homeowner know who to expect?
phrases.authorize_diagnostic_only: I'll authorise the diagnostic visit and the [amount] fee. For any repair over [threshold], the technician will need to call the homeowner before proceeding. Can you note that on the work order?
phrases.defer_membership_upsell: I appreciate you mentioning the membership program — I'll talk to the homeowner about that and we can sign up later if it's right. For today let's just get the visit on the schedule.
phrases.graceful_close_success: Great, so to confirm: technician [name if known] will arrive between [start] and [end] on [day] at [address]. Diagnostic fee is [amount], and any repair over [threshold] gets a callback before they start. Thanks!

tactics.name: Open with address + issue
tactics.script: Use `opener`. Many dispatchers can route the call faster if they know the service area and category up front.

tactics.name: Surface pricing immediately
tactics.script: Use `ask_diagnostic_fee` and `ask_after_hours`. A $79 diagnostic that's waived with repair is a different decision than a $149 fee that's separate.

tactics.name: Confirm window + arrival notification
tactics.script: Use `ask_arrival_window`. Push back on 'all-day' windows: 'Is there any way to get a tighter window? The homeowner has to plan around it.'

tactics.name: Set the diagnostic-only authorisation
tactics.script: Use `authorize_diagnostic_only`. This is the single most important line — it prevents the technician from doing a $1,200 repair on a $79 visit without homeowner sign-off.

tactics.name: Defer membership / service plan upsells
tactics.when: Dispatcher pitches a 'maintenance program' or 'annual service plan'.
tactics.script: Use `defer_membership_upsell`. These can be reasonable, but the homeowner shouldn't sign up over the phone without thinking.

boundaries: Do NOT authorise repairs sight-unseen. Diagnostic visit only; repairs require the homeowner's approval based on the tech's findings.
Do NOT commit to a maintenance plan or membership on the call.
Do NOT share the homeowner's full payment info — most contractors collect at the time of service.
Do NOT accept 'we'll get there when we can' as a window. Push for a 2-4 hour bracket minimum.
Do NOT schedule for a time the homeowner hasn't confirmed they can be there. A no-show may be billed.
success_signals: Dispatcher confirms a specific date + a tight window.
Dispatcher discloses diagnostic fee + after-hours policy.
Dispatcher notes the diagnostic-only authorisation on the work order.
Dispatcher offers technician's name + arrival notification method (text/call).
failure_signals: Earliest availability is past when the homeowner needs the issue addressed.
Diagnostic fee is non-refundable + non-credited toward repair.
Dispatcher won't commit to a tighter window than 'sometime that day'.

exit_strategy.on_success: Read back: day, arrival window, technician name (if known), diagnostic fee, repair-approval threshold, arrival notification method. Thank the dispatcher.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Note the deal-breaker (fee too high, window too wide, availability too far out). Thank the dispatcher. Report back so the homeowner can pick another contractor.
follow_ups: Email the homeowner: date, window, technician (if known), fee, repair-authorisation threshold.
Calendar the appointment with the address.
Calendar a reminder 30 minutes before the window starts (in case arrival-notification didn't fire).

required_user_info: One-line issue description + system age (if applicable)
Availability windows the homeowner can be on-site
Repair-authorisation threshold (dollar amount above which the homeowner wants to be called)
Whether to accept emergency / after-hours rates
